title: A robust computational framework for methylation age and disease-risk prediction based on pairwise learning
data_sources: NCBI GEO (multiple accessions: GSE55763, GSE72680, GSE132203, GSE87571, GSE210254, GSE210255, GSE196696, GSE147740, GSE152026, GSE56046, GSE220622, GSE87016, GSE203399, GSE69138, GSE76285, GSE199700, GSE62003, GSE197881, and others)

data_scope.total_samples_approx: more than 25,000 DNAm profiles across all tasks
data_scope.access: All datasets publicly available via NCBI GEO; code at github.com/Drizzle-Zhang/MAPLE

core_conclusion: MAPLE resolves the generalizability crisis of epigenetic clocks by using Siamese pairwise learning to predict relative age or disease-risk differences between sample pairs, encoding DNA methylation data into a batch-effect-free latent space. Across 31 benchmark tests spanning multiple sequencing platforms, preprocessing pipelines and 8 tissue types, MAPLE achieves a median absolute error of 1.6 years — outperforming five competing clocks. It further reaches mean AUROCs of 0.97 for CVD/T2D disease identification and 0.85 for pre-disease detection, enabling privacy-preserving clinical methylation-based risk assessment.
why_it_matters: MAPLE solves the batch-effect generalizability crisis of epigenetic clocks; converts the high-dimensional low-sample-size problem into a tractable one via quadratic pairwise expansion; extends clocks to pre-disease states (prediabetes, insulin resistance, subclinical atherosclerosis); respects cohort data-sharing constraints by training against released risk scores; ships as open-source software with all datasets traceable through public GEO accessions.
why_nature: General-purpose computational innovation (pairwise learning for batch-effect mitigation) transferable across omics; rigorous 31-benchmark, multi-platform, multi-tissue evaluation; bridges Siamese network architecture to clinical biomarkers for aging-related chronic disease; full reproducibility via code release and GEO accession traceability.
added_value_to_my_research: MAPLE embeddings as objective epigenetic health-outcome variable for place-based geospatial regression and 2SFCA demand weighting
Pairwise encoder principle for multi-source geospatial data fusion (mobile phone, remote sensing, social media) to wash out provider-specific offsets
Residual encoding paradigm (foundation model for structure + pairwise learning for residuals) validates approach for GeoAI spatial prediction fine-tuning
Integrated Gradients attribution pipeline as transferable template for feature-level explainability in spatial health prediction models
MAPLE pre-disease detection as equity lens — testing whether molecular diagnostics reach disadvantaged populations, complementing 2SFCA spatial accessibility analysis

related.title: Access to care affects electronic health record reliability and AI-driven disease prediction
related.relation: Health equity and access disparities in AI-driven disease prediction tools — mirrors MAPLE's pre-disease detection equity concerns

related.title: Integrative deep learning of spatial multi-omics with SWITCH
related.relation: Multi-source omics integration via deep learning — comparison baseline for pairwise batch-effect mitigation extended to spatial multi-omics

related.title: Predicting drug responses of unseen cell types through transfer learning with foundation models
related.relation: Transfer learning with foundation models for unseen biological contexts — natural pairing with MAPLE for disentangling genetic from environmental methylation

related.title: Evaluating deep learning for predicting epigenomic profiles
related.relation: Rigorous benchmarking of deep learning for epigenomic prediction — methodological complement to MAPLE's 31-benchmark evaluation

related.title: Decoding omics via representation learning
related.relation: Autoencoder representation learning across multi-modal omics — shares latent-space integration strategy with MAPLE's unified embedding approach

related.title: Foundation models in healthcare require rethinking reliability
related.relation: Reliability and equity of foundation models in clinical settings — directly relevant to evaluating equitable deployment of MAPLE-style methylation tools

figures.caption: Overview of MAPLE: data heterogeneity in clinical DNAm settings, two-step training pipeline (Siamese encoder then predictor), inference workflow, and pairwise learning strategies for age and disease risk tasks.

Performance heatmaps across 31 benchmark tests: (a) MAE across six blood datasets with three preprocessing methods; (b) MAE across seven non-blood tissue types — MAPLE achieves lowest mean MAE of 1.61 years.

GO pathway enrichment of MAPLE-identified age-related CpGs; sex-specific aging trajectories showing menopause-related acceleration at age ~52; age acceleration in Down syndrome, HIV, smoking, obesity and Alzheimer's disease cohorts.

CVD risk estimation: PCA embeddings by age and disease status; CVD risk score distributions (MAPLE vs. Cox model); AUROC/AUPRC for stroke (0.98/0.90), CAE, and AS; enriched CVD-related molecular pathways.

T2D risk estimation: PCA embeddings by age and disease status; T2D risk score distributions (MAPLE vs. Cox model); AUROC/AUPRC for T2D (0.96/0.71), prediabetes (0.89/0.48) and systemic insulin resistance (0.82/0.39); enriched T2D pathways.
